content.text: interface that every provider integration implements. Your chain or agent calls
on the interface; the underlying provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, a local Ollama server) handles the actual API call. Switching providers requires changing one constructor call — no other code changes.

attrs.alt: LangChain LLM interface hierarchy: BaseChatModel with ChatAnthropic, ChatOpenAI, and ChatOllama providers
attrs.title: LLM Interface Hierarchy

content.text:
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.messages import SystemMessage, HumanMessage

# Basic invocation
llm = ChatAnthropic(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=1024,
    temperature=0.2,
)

response = llm.invoke([
    SystemMessage(content="You are a Python expert."),
    HumanMessage(content="What is a decorator?"),
])
print(response.content)           # str
print(response.usage_metadata)    # {"input_tokens": ..., "output_tokens": ...}

# Streaming
for chunk in llm.stream([HumanMessage(content="Count to 5.")]):
    print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)
print()

# Async
import asyncio

async def call_async():
    result = await llm.ainvoke([HumanMessage(content="Hello")])
    return result.content

print(asyncio.run(call_async()))
Every chat model takes a list of typed message objects. The type determines how the model treats the content:

from langchain_core.messages import (
    SystemMessage,    # model behaviour / persona
    HumanMessage,     # user turn
    AIMessage,        # model turn (for multi-turn history)
    ToolMessage,      # result of a tool call
)

# Multi-turn conversation history
conversation = [
    SystemMessage(content="You are a helpful assistant."),
    HumanMessage(content="What is 2 + 2?"),
    AIMessage(content="4"),
    HumanMessage(content="And multiply that by 3?"),
]

response = llm.invoke(conversation)
print(response.content)  # "12"

# AIMessage carries tool call requests when tools are bound
# ToolMessage carries the tool execution result back to the model
from langchain_core.messages import ToolMessage

tool_result_msg = ToolMessage(
    content='{"temperature": 22, "unit": "celsius"}',
    tool_call_id="call_abc123",  # must match the AIMessage tool_call id
)
to declare what functions the model can request the caller to execute. The model returns tool call requests inside the
method instructs the model to return a Pydantic object directly, using tool-calling under the hood:

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Literal


class Sentiment(BaseModel):
    label: Literal["positive", "neutral", "negative"]
    score: float = Field(ge=0.0, le=1.0, description="Confidence 0-1")
    reason: str


structured_llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001").with_structured_output(Sentiment)

result: Sentiment = structured_llm.invoke(
    "Classify the sentiment: 'I love LangChain, it makes my life so much easier!'"
)
print(result.label)   # "positive"
print(result.score)   # float
print(result.reason)  # str
, swapping providers is a single-line change. The rest of your chain code stays identical:

# All three are drop-in replacements for each other in a chain
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_ollama import ChatOllama

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Explain {topic} briefly.")

# Swap this one line to change provider
llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
# llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
# llm = ChatOllama(model="llama3.2")

chain = prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()
print(chain.invoke({"topic": "embeddings"}))

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.rate_limiters import InMemoryRateLimiter

# Limit to 10 requests per second
rate_limiter = InMemoryRateLimiter(
    requests_per_second=10,
    check_every_n_seconds=0.1,
    max_bucket_size=10,
)

llm = ChatAnthropic(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    rate_limiter=rate_limiter,
    max_retries=3,       # auto-retry on 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff
    timeout=30,          # seconds per request
)

# Batch calls — rate limiter automatically throttles across the batch
results = llm.batch([
    [HumanMessage(content=f"Item {i}")]
    for i in range(50)
])
print(f"Got {len(results)} results")

from langchain_core.callbacks import UsageMetadataCallbackHandler

# Track cumulative token usage across multiple calls
usage_callback = UsageMetadataCallbackHandler()

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

for topic in ["LLMs", "agents", "RAG"]:
    llm.invoke(
        [HumanMessage(content=f"Define {topic} in one sentence.")],
        config={"callbacks": [usage_callback]},
    )

print(usage_callback.usage_metadata)
# {"input_tokens": 45, "output_tokens": 60, "total_tokens": 105}
Caching is essential for reducing cost and latency during development and for idempotent production pipelines. LangChain provides an in-memory cache and an SQLite-backed cache:

from langchain_core.globals import set_llm_cache
from langchain_core.caches import InMemoryCache
from langchain_community.cache import SQLiteCache

# In-memory — cleared on process restart
set_llm_cache(InMemoryCache())

# SQLite — persists across restarts (useful in development)
set_llm_cache(SQLiteCache(database_path=".langchain_cache.db"))

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

# First call hits the API
r1 = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content="What is 1+1?")])
# Second identical call returns from cache — no API call, no tokens consumed
r2 = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content="What is 1+1?")])
assert r1.content == r2.content

# Disable caching for a specific call
r3 = llm.invoke(
    [HumanMessage(content="What is 1+1?")],
    config={"cache": False},
)
Custom System Prompts and Partial Variables
Pre-fill part of the prompt at construction time using
so callers only need to supply the remaining variables:

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from datetime import date

# Template with two variables
base_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Today is {date}. You are a {role}."),
    ("human", "{question}"),
])

# Partially fill date and role at chain construction time
expert_prompt = base_prompt.partial(
    date=date.today().isoformat(),
    role="Python expert",
)

# Callers only need to pass {question}
chain = expert_prompt | ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001") | StrOutputParser()
result = chain.invoke({"question": "What are type hints?"})
print(result)
in unit tests to avoid real API calls. It cycles through a pre-configured list of responses:

import pytest
from langchain_core.language_models.fake_chat_models import FakeListChatModel
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate


def make_chain(llm):
    prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Answer: {question}")
    return prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()


def test_chain_returns_first_response():
    fake_llm = FakeListChatModel(responses=["Paris", "London"])
    chain = make_chain(fake_llm)
    result = chain.invoke({"question": "Capital of France?"})
    assert result == "Paris"


def test_chain_cycles_responses():
    fake_llm = FakeListChatModel(responses=["A", "B"])
    chain = make_chain(fake_llm)
    assert chain.invoke({"question": "q1"}) == "A"
    assert chain.invoke({"question": "q2"}) == "B"
    assert chain.invoke({"question": "q3"}) == "A"  # cycles back


def test_batch_uses_fake_responses():
    fake_llm = FakeListChatModel(responses=["ans1", "ans2", "ans3"])
    chain = make_chain(fake_llm)
    results = chain.batch([{"question": f"q{i}"} for i in range(3)])
    assert results == ["ans1", "ans2", "ans3"]
Model Comparison: Choosing the Right Tier

from langchain_core.globals import set_llm_cache
from langchain_core.caches import InMemoryCache

# In-memory — cleared on process restart

# SQLite — persists across restarts (useful in development)
set_llm_cache(SQLiteCache(database_path=".langchain_cache.db"))

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

# First call hits the API
r1 = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content="What is 1+1?")])
# Second identical call returns from cache — no API call, no tokens consumed

assert r1.content == r2.content

# Disable caching for a specific call
r3 = llm.invoke(
    [HumanMessage(content="What is 1+1?")],
    config={"cache": False},
)

so callers only need to supply the remaining variables:

in unit tests to avoid real API calls. It cycles through a pre-configured list of responses:

import pytest
from langchain_core.language_models.fake_chat_models import FakeListChatModel
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate


def make_chain(llm):
    prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Answer: {question}")
    return prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()


def test_chain_returns_first_response():
    fake_llm = FakeListChatModel(responses=["Paris", "London"])
    chain = make_chain(fake_llm)
    result = chain.invoke({"question": "Capital of France?"})
    assert result == "Paris"


def test_chain_cycles_responses():
    fake_llm = FakeListChatModel(responses=["A", "B"])
    chain = make_chain(fake_llm)
    assert chain.invoke({"question": "q1"}) == "A"
    assert chain.invoke({"question": "q2"}) == "B"
    assert chain.invoke({"question": "q3"}) == "A"  # cycles back


def test_batch_uses_fake_responses():
    fake_llm = FakeListChatModel(responses=["ans1", "ans2", "ans3"])
    chain = make_chain(fake_llm)
    results = chain.batch([{"question": f"q{i}"} for i in range(3)])
    assert results == ["ans1", "ans2", "ans3"]
Model Comparison: Choosing the Right Tier
LangSmith provides end-to-end tracing for every LLM call, chain step, and tool invocation. Enable it by setting environment variables — no code changes required:

import os

# Set before importing LangChain
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2"] = "true"
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_API_KEY"] = "your-langsmith-key"
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_PROJECT"] = "my-project"

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

# This call is automatically traced — no decorator or callback needed
response = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content="Explain tracing in one sentence.")])
print(response.content)
# View the trace at: https://smith.langchain.com/o/.../projects/p/my-project

# Tag specific runs for filtering in the UI
response = llm.invoke(
    [HumanMessage(content="Another call")],
    config={"tags": ["production", "user-facing"], "metadata": {"user_id": "user-42"}},
)
print(response.content)
For high-throughput pipelines, combine async invocation with
to run many requests concurrently without hitting synchronous

import asyncio
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
from langchain_core.rate_limiters import InMemoryRateLimiter

rate_limiter = InMemoryRateLimiter(requests_per_second=5)
llm = ChatAnthropic(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    rate_limiter=rate_limiter,
)

queries = [
    "Define machine learning",
    "Define deep learning",
    "Define reinforcement learning",
    "Define transfer learning",
    "Define federated learning",
]


async def run_all(queries: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    tasks = [
        llm.ainvoke([HumanMessage(content=q)])
        for q in queries
    ]
    responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return [r.content for r in responses]


results = asyncio.run(run_all(queries))
for q, r in zip(queries, results):
    print(f"{q}: {r[:60]}...")
